data.history: The Al Olema family (also known as Sheikh Sultan Al Ulama, the Fikree, and the Al Khaldi family) are first cousins of the Al Farooq family, a prominent Sunni Muslim business family. The Al Farooq family originated from Saudi Arabia with their ancestral home later in Bastak in the Hormozgan province of southern Iran before migrating to Dubai in the first wave of migration starting in 1899. This migration was prompted by the introduction of customs tax by the Persian government under the Qajar dynasty, political instability in 1909, and a flu epidemic in 1918. Given the close family relationship between the Al Olema and Al Farooq families, it is likely they shared similar migration patterns and business interests in the Persian Gulf region.
data.description: The Al Olema family are first cousins of the prominent Al Farooq merchant family and are also known by several names including Sheikh Sultan Al Ulama, the Fikree, and the Al Khaldi family.

data.history: The Al Qurayyat family's documented history is intricately linked to the Al-Jouf region and the historical settlement of Qurayyat (Qurayyat al-Salt). According to the research, Muhsin al-Shaalan, who was Nuri Al Shalaan's cousin, ceded the Qurayyat region to the Saudi state, representing the last remaining part of the al-Jawf region to be incorporated into Saudi Arabia. This cession occurred during the early formation period of the modern Saudi state, when territorial boundaries were being established through negotiations and conflicts with neighboring powers. The Emirate of Transjordan initially rejected this arrangement, demanding the establishment of a neutral zone and other territorial concessions, leading to tensions that required British intervention. The conflict escalated to the point where Saudi forces advanced toward Jordanian territory, reaching as far as Yadodah near Amman, before British forces bombed Saudi positions and forced a withdrawal. The resolution came through the Hada agreement between Saudi Arabia and Britain (representing Transjordan), which formally recognized Saudi control over the Qurayyat region. Following this incorporation, the area was initially known as the 'Emirate of Qurayyat and the Northern Border Inspectorate.' The region's administrative center was later moved from the village of Kaf to the village of Nabak, which subsequently became known as the City of Qurayyat.
data.description: The Al Qurayyat are associated with the Qurayyat region in Al-Jouf Province, northern Saudi Arabia, with their territorial control formally transferred to Saudi state through Muhsin al-Shaalan's cession in the early 20th century.
data.nameEtymology: The name derives from 'Qurayyat al-Salt,' referring to the specific region that was the last part of al-Jawf to be incorporated into the Saudi state

data.history: The Al Wakil family name appears in multiple contexts across the Gulf region and Islamic world, reflecting its Arabic linguistic origins meaning 'representative' or 'deputy.' The research reveals several distinct lineages using this name. In the architectural field, Abdel-Wahed El-Wakil (born 1943) represents a prominent Egyptian branch who became renowned for Islamic architecture, particularly designing over 15 mosques in Saudi Arabia from the 1970s onward. In Australia, Susan Wakil (1933-2018) was born in Bessarabia and fled to Australia in 1949 with her aunt, later marrying Isaac Wakil who was born in Baghdad in 1927 and came to Australia after the 1941 Farhud violence. The term 'wakil' itself has deep Islamic legal significance, referring to deputies who act on behalf of Muslim rulers and receive Bay'ah, with the concept being fundamental to Islamic governance structures across various Muslim empires including the Mughal, Safavid, and Ottoman systems.

Ar Rass itself has ancient origins, with its name meaning 'an old well' in Arabic, and was mentioned in poetry by Hassan Bin Thabit, a companion of Prophet Muhammad. The city has historically served as an important resource center for Arab tribes in the Najd peninsula and Al-Qassim region. After periods of tribal migration and settlement, including the departure of Bani Asad tribes and their replacement by Banu Tamim and Banu Lam tribes, the area experienced renewed settlement around 950 AH when groups returned from places like Ishaqir to rebuild the town. data.history: The Al Mohsen family name derives from the Arabic name Muhsin/Mohsen, meaning 'the one who beautifies or improves, particularly one's worship of or relationship with God' and can mean helper, attractive, beneficent, benefactor, and charitable. The name comes from the Arabic triconsonantal root Ḥ-S-N meaning 'beauty, beautiful, benevolence, benevolent, excellence, excellent.' The first person known to have the name 'Muhsin' was Muhsin ibn Ali, the son of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah bint Muhammad. The research reveals several notable individuals with variations of this name across the Middle East, including prominent religious scholars like Mohsen al-Amin al-Amili (1867-1952), a Lebanese Shia cleric who became a reference for Shia Muslims in Damascus and established important educational institutions. The name appears in various forms - Mohsen, Mohsin, Muhsin - across different regions and has been carried by military officers, politicians, scholars, and business figures throughout Islamic history. In Lebanon, the name is associated with the Jabal Mohsen neighborhood in Tripoli, which houses a significant Alawite population and has been a center of sectarian conflict with the neighboring Sunni area of Bab al-Tabbaneh.
data.description: Al Mohsen is an Arabic family name derived from the root meaning 'beneficent' or 'one who improves,' historically associated with religious scholarship and carried by notable figures across the Islamic world.

data.history:
The Al Qemzi (also known as Qumzan) are a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia. The Bani Yas confederation, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 in present-day Abu Dhabi territory. As part of this confederation, the Al Qemzi were among the constituent tribes that formed one of the largest tribal groups in what is now the United Arab Emirates.

The Bani Yas confederation, including the Al Qemzi, experienced significant demographic changes throughout the 20th century. From an estimated 12,000 members in the early 1900s, the population declined to approximately 8,000 by the early 1950s, largely due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment in oil and construction sectors. The 1968 Abu Dhabi census recorded 5,884 Bani Yas members within the emirate, though tribal affiliations were not tracked in subsequent censuses.

As members of the Bani Yas confederation, the Al Qemzi were part of a complex tribal alliance that included the ruling families of both Abu Dhabi (Al Nahyan from Al Bu Falah) and Dubai (Al Maktoum from Al Bu Falasah). The confederation's influence extended from Abu Dhabi to Dubai and even to Khor Al Adaid in southeastern Qatar, where Bani Yas members settled in 1835.
data.description: The Al Qemzi (Qumzan) are a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation that originated in Najd and settled in the UAE region around 1700. They are part of one of the most influential tribal confederations in the Emirates.
data.originStory: The Al Qemzi, as part of the Bani Yas confederation, originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. They settled with other Bani Yas clans in the Liwa oasis around 1700.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary members of the Bani Yas confederation identify the Al Qemzi as one of more than 20 sections forming part of the confederation in modern times.

The Al Fadhli family name (Arabic: الفضلي) derives from the adjective أفضل (ʾafḍal) meaning 'best' or 'most outstanding' and is most commonly found in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia. The name is historically connected to the Fadhli Sultanate, an independent sultanate that existed on the southern coast of the Arabian Peninsula from the 17th century until 1967. The Fadhli Sultanate was located northeast of Aden, extending for a hundred miles along the coast from the eastern limits of Aden to the western boundary of the Aulaqi at Maqatin. British sources described the Fadhli as 'one of the most powerful and warlike tribes near Aden.' The sultanate's history includes various rulers dating back to at least Othman who ruled until 1670, through a succession of sultans until the last sultan, Nasser bin Abdullah bin Hussein bin Ahmed al-Fadhli, was deposed in 1967 upon the founding of the People's Republic of South Yemen. The sultanate signed protection agreements with Great Britain in the early 20th century and became part of the British Aden Protectorate as one of the original 'Nine Cantons.' Modern bearers of the Al Fadhli name include notable figures in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, particularly in sports, with several Kuwaiti footballers carrying this surname.
data.description: Al Fadhli is an Arabic surname most commonly found in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, historically connected to the powerful Fadhli Sultanate that ruled parts of southern Arabian Peninsula from the 17th century until 1967.

data.history: Based on available evidence, the Al Dakheel name appears in historical records across different contexts in the Arabian Peninsula. In July 2003, Ahmad Al-Dakheel was killed during a police raid on a farm outside of Al-Qasim, identified as one of four Saudi nationals involved in militant activities. In July 2004, Bandar Al-Dakheel was reported to have escaped during a shootout east of Riyadh where Abdullah ibn Ahmed Al-Rashoud was killed. Additionally, in December 2004, Faisal Al-Dakheel was killed in Riyadh during anti-government activities, listed among the Kingdom's 26 Most Wanted individuals. A separate reference mentions Sheikha Samira Hamed Dakheel as the mother of historian Hatoon al-Fassi, described as belonging to 'the branch of the Hijazi tribe of Harb that resided in Jeddah.' The surname also appears in connection with Ibrahim al-Mujab's descendants, where 'Sayyid Dakheel ibn Ibrahim ibn Khalaf' is mentioned as an ancestor of the Abu-Ragheef tribe. However, these references appear to represent different family lines rather than a unified Al Dakheel family structure.
data.description: The Al Dakheel name appears in various historical contexts across the Arabian Peninsula, with documented presence in different regions and time periods, though no unified family narrative emerges from available sources.

data.history: The research reveals two distinct family lineages that may be confused due to similar names: Al Bouhamad and Al-Balushi (also spelled Al Bloshi). The Al Bouhamad family represents an Ahsa'i family that migrated to Kuwait in the late 19th century. This family established deep religious roots in Kuwait, founding their own Hussainiya in 1952 known as Hussainiyat Al Buhamad. The Al-Balushi surname, alternatively spelled Bloushi, Blooshi, or Beloshi, represents an entirely different lineage - an Arabized form of Balochi, indicating Baloch ancestry from Balochistan. Al-Balushi families trace their ancestral origins to the Makran coast of Balochistan and primarily migrated to the Gulf states in the 18th century. These families are predominantly Sunni Muslim and represent one of the largest ethnic groups in Oman, making up an estimated 35% of the population. The confusion may stem from similar transliterations, but these represent distinct tribal and ethnic origins.
data.description: Research indicates potential confusion between Al Bouhamad (Ahsa'i family from late 19th century Kuwait) and Al-Balushi (Baloch families from 18th century Makran coast). These represent separate lineages with different origins, religions, and migration patterns.
data.originStory: Al-Balushi families originated from the Makran coast of Balochistan (present-day Pakistan/Iran) and migrated to Gulf states primarily in the 18th century. Al Bouhamad represents a separate Ahsa'i family lineage that migrated to Kuwait in the late 19th century.
data.modernStatus: Al-Balushi families are well-established across Gulf states in various sectors including sports, politics, and business. Al Bouhamad family maintains strong religious institutional presence in Kuwait through their Hussainiya.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Balushi is an Arabized form of the term Balochi or Baluchi, denoting Baloch ancestry from Balochistan.

data.history: The Al Duhaim family appears in historical records across different regions of the Middle East. In Tubna, Syria, Al-Duhaim is listed among the Melkite Greek Catholic families in this village in the Hauran region, which has inhabitants descended from Ghassanid Christian tribes that immigrated from Yemen to southern Syria in the early 3rd century CE. The Ghassanids established the Kingdom of the Ghassanids in this region and maintained a presence there through the Byzantine period. In Kuwait, Abdul Latif bin Ibrahim Al-Duhaim played a pioneering role in the country's pharmaceutical history, establishing the first private pharmacy in Kuwait in 1927 under the name Islamic Charitable Pharmacy. This pharmacy sold imported medicines from India, Basra and Baghdad, marking an important development in Kuwait's healthcare infrastructure during the early 20th century.

data.history: The Al Qaed family name appears in the research primarily in the context of Qaed Salim Sinan al-Harithi (1955-2002), a Yemeni militant leader who was born in Shabwah Governorate, Yemen. Al-Harithi was a member of the Banu al-Harith tribe and moved to Marib in North Yemen as a young man due to the South Yemen government's hostility towards tribal customs in the late 1960s and 1970s. During the 1980s, he traveled to Afghanistan to fight against the Soviet Union, where he developed a relationship with Osama bin Laden. After returning to Yemen, he operated a family farm in Shabwah and later became involved with Islamic militant activities. The research does not provide information about the broader Al Qaed family history, business activities, or political evolution beyond this individual's militant activities, which culminated in his death in a U.S. drone strike in 2002.

data.history: The research provided contains information about three individuals with the surname 'Amer' from different backgrounds, but none represent a coherent Gulf family lineage. Mohamed Abdel Hakim Amer (1919-1967) was an Egyptian military officer from Samalut in El Minya, Egypt, who served as Egypt's Defense Minister and Vice President under Gamal Abdel Nasser. Mohammed Mustafa Amer (born 1981) is a Palestinian-American comedian whose family fled Kuwait during the Gulf War when he was nine years old, settling in Houston, Texas. His father worked as an engineer for the Kuwait Oil Company, but the family's connection to Kuwait was professional rather than ancestral. Huda Ben Amer is a former Libyan politician from Marj near Benghazi who served under Muammar Gaddafi's regime. These individuals appear to be unrelated and represent different nationalities and backgrounds rather than a unified Gulf family with shared origins or tribal connections.

data.history: The Rajab family research reveals several distinct branches with different origins and trajectories. The most documented branch includes the Palestinian Rajab family, exemplified by Hind Rami Iyad Rajab (2018-2024), a five-year-old Palestinian girl killed during the Gaza war in January 2024. Her tragic death gained international attention and sparked investigations into Israeli military actions. Another prominent branch is the Kuwaiti Rajab family, represented by Tareq Al-Sayid Rajab who began collecting artifacts in the 1940s as a teenager in Baghdad, later establishing Kuwait's first Islamic Art museum in 1980. This branch became prominent in Kuwait's cultural and educational sectors through the founding of the New English School and the Tareq Rajab Museum. A third notable branch is the Bahraini Rajab family, with Nabeel Rajab (born 1964) serving as a prominent human rights activist and president of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights, facing multiple imprisonments for his activism during the 2011 Bahraini uprising and beyond.
data.description: The research reveals multiple Rajab family branches across the Middle East, including Palestinian, Kuwaiti, and Bahraini lines, with members prominent in tragedy, cultural preservation, and human rights activism respectively.
data.modernStatus: The Kuwaiti Rajab branch operates the Tareq Rajab Museum, fully funded by the family with third-generation involvement. The Bahraini branch remains active in human rights advocacy through Nabeel Rajab's continued activism. The Palestinian branch gained international recognition through tragedy during the Gaza conflict.

data.history: The Mushaima family is a Bahraini family with documented presence in the country during the modern era. Based on the research, members of the family have been involved in Bahrain's political and social developments, particularly during the 2011 Bahraini uprising. The family includes individuals who participated in pro-democracy demonstrations and faced persecution by the Bahraini government. Ali Abdulhadi Saleh Jafar Mushaima became the first fatality of the 2011 Bahraini uprising on February 14, 2011, when he was shot by security forces in Al Daih. Other family members, including Sami Mushaima and Hasan Mushaima, were also involved in opposition activities, with Sami Mushaima being executed in 2017 after being convicted on terrorism charges that human rights organizations claim were based on forced confessions extracted through torture. The family's experiences reflect the broader Shia community's struggles in Bahrain, including alleged systematic discrimination and persecution by the Sunni-led government.
data.description: A Bahraini family that gained prominence during the 2011 uprising, with multiple members involved in pro-democracy activities and facing government persecution, including executions.
data.modernStatus: The family has been significantly impacted by government crackdowns, with several members executed, imprisoned, or forced into exile due to their opposition activities.

data.history: The Al Wazir family prominently emerged in Palestinian political history through Khalil Ibrahim al-Wazir, born in 1935 in Ramla, Palestine, to Muslim parents. His father Ibrahim al-Wazir worked as a grocer in the city. The family became refugees when they were expelled from Ramla in July 1948 during the Arab-Israeli War, settling in the Bureij refugee camp in Gaza Strip. From this displacement emerged one of the most influential Palestinian political figures of the 20th century. Khalil al-Wazir co-founded Fatah between 1959-1960 with Yasser Arafat and other Palestinian exiles, becoming a key architect of Palestinian resistance. He established Fatah's first bureau in Algeria in 1962 and commanded the organization's armed wing al-Assifa. His influence extended across the Arab world as he built connections with Communist regimes and third-world leaders, opening diplomatic channels from China to Cuba. The family's political legacy continued through his wife Intissar al-Wazir, who became the first female member of Fatah in 1959 and later served as Minister of Social Affairs in the Palestinian National Authority. Their son Jihad al-Wazir became governor of the Palestinian Monetary Authority, maintaining the family's prominence in Palestinian governance and finance.
data.description: A prominent Palestinian political family originating from Ramla, best known for Khalil al-Wazir (Abu Jihad), co-founder of Fatah and key PLO leader, and his wife Intissar, a pioneering female politician in Palestinian governance.
data.originStory: The Al Wazir family lived in Ramla, Palestine under British Mandatory rule, where Ibrahim al-Wazir worked as a grocer. The family's modern political trajectory began with their forced displacement during the 1948 Arab-Israeli War, when they were expelled along with 50,000-70,000 other Palestinians from Lydda and Ramla, settling as refugees in Gaza Strip.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant influence in Palestinian politics and international finance, with Intissar al-Wazir having served as Minister of Social Affairs and head of the Palestinian Authority Martyrs Fund, while their son Jihad works for the International Monetary Fund after serving as governor of the Palestinian Monetary Authority.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Wazir derives from the Arabic word 'wazir' meaning vizier or minister, a high-ranking political advisor position historically used across the Islamic world. The term comes from Arabic 'wazara' meaning 'to bear a burden' or possibly from Persian 'vičir' meaning decree or judge.

data.history: The Al Maktoum family, also known as Bin Rashid, belongs to the Al Bu Falasah tribe, which is a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation. The family established itself as the ruling house of Dubai, with documented rule beginning with Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum (Saeed II), who ruled until 1958. Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum acceded as ruler following his father's death on 10 September 1958, having effectively managed Dubai's affairs since the late 1930s. Under his leadership, Dubai transformed from a small settlement around Dubai Creek into a modern port city and regional commercial hub, particularly through gold trading with India and major infrastructure projects like the dredging of Dubai Creek and construction of Port Rashid. Rashid was instrumental in the formation of the UAE, meeting with Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan in 1968 at Argoub Al Sedirah to establish the union through what became known as 'the only nation founded on a handshake.' Upon his death in 1990, his son Maktoum bin Rashid became ruler until 2006, when Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum assumed power and continues as ruler of Dubai, Vice President and Prime Minister of the UAE.
data.description: The ruling family of Dubai, belonging to the Al Bu Falasah tribe of Bani Yas, who transformed Dubai from a small trading settlement into a major international commercial and tourism hub.
data.originStory: The Al Maktoum family traces its origins to the Al Bu Falasah tribe, which is considered one of the branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation that historically controlled this region and was known for its elevated status and influence in the area.

data.modernStatus: Currently the ruling family of Dubai with Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum serving as Vice President and Prime Minister of UAE and Ruler of Dubai. The family oversees Dubai's position as a global commercial, financial, and tourism center, owns Godolphin Stables horse racing empire, and maintains significant business interests through various holding companies.

data.history: The Al Fadala family is descended from the ancient Arab Tayy tribe and historically resided in Umm Qarn, Qatar. According to local tradition documented in Wikipedia sources, Umm Qarn is considered the ancestral homeland of the Al Mudahika tribe, with the Al Fadala family also having historical residence there. The village of Umm Qarn is located in eastern Qatar, about 35 kilometers from the capital Doha, in the northern part of Al Daayen Municipality. The area is characterized by small hills and valleys with elevations ranging from 3 to 45 meters. The family's presence in this region appears to be of longstanding historical significance, though specific dates of settlement or migration are not provided in the available research.

data.history: The Al Maamari family appears to be an established Omani family with documented members across multiple fields including literature, journalism, human rights activism, politics, and construction. The most prominent documented figure is Suleiman Al Maamari (born 1974), an accomplished Omani writer and journalist who served as head of The Omani Society for Writers and Literates between 2008-2010 and currently works as head of cultural programs at Sultanate of Oman Radio. Another notable member is Talib Al Mamari (born 1972), a former parliamentarian who served in the Omani Consultative Assembly from 2011-2013 and became a recognized human rights defender after his arrest following environmental protests in Liwa. The family also includes Moussa Abdel Karim Al-Maamari (born 1931), who single-handedly constructed Moussa Castle between Deir el Qamar and Beit ed-Dine in Lebanon, demonstrating the family's presence beyond Oman. Additional family members include Dr. Mohammed bin Saeed Al Maamari, who received the second class Order of Oman in 2024, and cameraman Rabih Maamari who worked for Al Mayadeen TV and was killed in an Israeli strike in Lebanon in November 2023.
data.description: An established Omani family with documented members in literature, journalism, human rights activism, politics, and construction, spanning multiple generations across the Gulf and Levant regions.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions in Omani civil society, government, and media, with members serving in cultural institutions, receiving state honors, and engaging in political activism.

data.history: The Al Zubaidi family traces its origins to multiple historical lineages connected to early Islamic history. The most prominent modern figure is Aidarus Qassem Abdulaziz Al-Zubaidi, born in 1967 in Zubayd village in the mountains of Dhale Governorate, South Yemen. His family belongs to the Zubayd-Himyar tribe. The research indicates connections to the ancient Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a prominent companion of Prophet Muhammad from the 7th century, though the exact genealogical links between the historical figure and modern Al Zubaidi families are not definitively established in the provided sources. The Al-Obaidi branch mentioned in Iraq represents a separate lineage that claims descent from the Zubaid tribe, having migrated from Najd around the 1750s under Sultan Jabr bin Maktoum Al Zubaidi. The modern Al Zubaidi family of Yemen has been significantly involved in South Yemeni politics, particularly through Aidarus al-Zubaidi's leadership roles including governor of Aden (2015-2017) and chairman of the Southern Transitional Council since 2017.
data.description: A family name with multiple lineages, most notably represented by Yemeni political leader Aidarus al-Zubaidi of the Zubayd-Himyar tribe, with historical connections claimed to early Islamic figures.
data.originStory: According to the research, the Al-Zubaidi surname connects to multiple origins. The Iraqi Al-Obaidi branch claims descent from Zubaid tribe members who were former Arabian royalty ruling a sultanate in Najd for over 800 years before migration to Iraq around the 1750s. The Yemeni branch, represented by Aidarus al-Zubaidi, belongs to the Zubayd-Himyar tribe from South Yemen. Historical connections are claimed to Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a 7th-century companion of Prophet Muhammad.

data.history: The Al Yarubi family was a prominent Omani dynasty that ruled as Imams of Oman from 1624 to 1744, representing one of the most significant periods in Omani history. The dynasty came to power during a time when Oman was establishing itself as a major maritime and trading power in the Arabian Peninsula and Indian Ocean region. The Al Yarubi period was characterized by territorial expansion, naval supremacy, and the construction of numerous fortifications throughout Oman. However, by the 1740s, the dynasty had weakened considerably, with the last Imam, Saif bin Sultan II, losing control to Persian forces led by Nader Shah in 1737-1744. The dynasty's rule effectively ended in 1749 when Bal'arab bin Himyar, the last Yarubi claimant to the Imamate, was defeated and killed by Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi, who founded the Al Bu Said dynasty that continues to rule Oman today. The Al Yarubi legacy lives on through the numerous forts and architectural achievements built during their reign, many of which are mentioned in historical records as having been constructed or expanded during the Al-Yarubi period between 1624 and 1744.
data.description: The Al Yarubi was an Omani ruling dynasty that served as Imams of Oman from 1624 to 1744, known for their maritime expansion and fort construction. Their rule ended when the last claimant, Bal'arab bin Himyar, was defeated by Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi in 1749.
data.modernStatus: The Al Yarubi dynasty no longer holds political power, having been replaced by the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1749. However, their architectural and cultural legacy remains significant in Oman, with many forts built during their reign still standing as tourist attractions and UNESCO World Heritage sites.

data.history: The research provides only minimal direct information about the Al Sawafi family. The primary historical reference appears in the context of the 850-855 Armenian Revolt against Abbasid authority, where an Arab military commander named al-Ali as-Sawafi (emir al-Ali as-Sawafi) is mentioned. According to the Abbasid historian Al-Baladhuri, as-Sawafi was sent by Abu Sa'id al-Marwazi to subdue the Armenian provinces of Taron and Vaspurakan under the pretext of raising taxes. One of the immediate causes of the Armenian revolt was vengeance for as-Sawafi's desecration of the Dayr al-Aqdah monastery in Sisakan, which caused outrage among Armenian princes and prompted rebellion. When as-Sawafi entered Vaspurakan, he was defeated and pushed out by ishkhan Ashot I Artsruni. In the context of Islamic taxation, the term 'sawafi' historically referred to lands that became the personal domains of the caliph - specifically properties that Mu'awiya acquired in Medina from its inhabitants, often purchased at significantly below market value during times of hunger and desperation. These sawafi lands were distinct from typical conquered state property and became a source of economic grievance that contributed to the Battle of al-Harra in 683 CE.
data.description: The Al Sawafi family has limited documented historical presence, with the main reference being a 9th century Arab military commander involved in the suppression of the Armenian Revolt of 850-855 CE.
data.modernStatus: One contemporary reference shows Sheikh Hamoud bin Hamid bin Hamad Al-Sawafi receiving the Order of Royal Commendation (Second Class) from Sultan of Oman in 2023, indicating continued family presence in Oman.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Sawafi' derives from the Arabic term referring to lands that became the personal domains of caliphs, particularly properties acquired by Mu'awiya in Medina that were purchased at below-market prices during periods of economic distress.

data.history: The Al Farisi family name derives from Salman al-Farisi (Salman the Persian), born Ruzbeh Khoshnudan around 568-570 AD, who was one of the closest companions of Prophet Muhammad. Originally from either Kazerun in Fars province or Isfahan, Salman was born into a Zoroastrian family and spent his first sixteen years studying to become a magus before converting first to Christianity and then to Islam after meeting Muhammad in Medina. He became the first Persian to convert to Islam and was so revered that Muhammad declared him 'one of the People of the House.' After Muhammad's death in 632, Salman participated in the Muslim conquest of Persia and became the first governor of the Sasanid capital Ctesiphon under Caliph Umar ibn Al-Khattab. He is also credited with translating the Quran into Persian, making him the first person to interpret and translate the Quran into a foreign language. Salman died around 652-656 CE, with his tomb located in the Salman Al-Farsi Mosque in Al-Mada'in, though some sources place it in Isfahan or Jerusalem. The Al Farisi name thus represents one of the most ancient and prestigious lineages in Islamic history, tracing back to one of the Prophet's most trusted companions.
data.description: Al Farisi traces its lineage to Salman al-Farisi (Salman the Persian), one of Prophet Muhammad's closest companions and the first Persian convert to Islam. The family name honors this revered figure who served as governor of Ctesiphon and was the first to translate the Quran into Persian.
data.originStory: The Al Farisi lineage originates from Salman al-Farisi, born Ruzbeh Khoshnudan in ancient Persia around 568-570 AD. Born into a Zoroastrian family in either Kazerun or Isfahan, he dedicated sixteen years to studying as a Zoroastrian priest before encountering Christianity and eventually converting to Islam after meeting Prophet Muhammad in Medina. His spiritual journey from Zoroastrianism through Christianity to Islam, combined with his close relationship with Muhammad, established him as one of the most revered figures in early Islamic history.

data.history: The research provided contains information about three distinct entities named Kasab/Qasab, but none relate to a Gulf family called 'Al Kasab.' The first is Muhammad Ajmal Amir Kasab, a Pakistani terrorist from the Qassab community (traditionally butchers) who participated in the 2008 Mumbai attacks. He was born in Faridkot village, Okara District, Punjab, Pakistan, to a family of modest means - his father ran a snack cart. The second reference is to the 2008 Mumbai attacks themselves, where Kasab was the sole surviving attacker who was captured, tried, and executed in 2012. The third reference is to Kessab, a town in northwestern Syria with a majority Armenian population, completely unrelated to any Gulf family lineage. No information exists in the provided research about an Al Kasab family from the Gulf region, their origins, tribal affiliations, business activities, or historical presence in the GCC states.

data.history: The Al Jabir family discussed in this research refers to the Al-Sabah ruling family of Kuwait, specifically the Al-Jaber branch. The House of Al-Sabah originates from the Bani Utbah confederation and settled in what is now Kuwait around the mid-1700s. According to oral traditions, they were previously expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to predatory habits involving caravans and trading ships. Another tradition states they fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south, then returned and moved to Zubara on Qatar's west coast before finally settling in Kuwait where they found water. The critical alliance between Kuwait and Britain was forged during the reign of Mubarak the Great (1896–1915), establishing Kuwait's modern political foundations. The Al-Jaber branch has provided several rulers including Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (10th ruler, 1921–1950), Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah (13th ruler, 1977–2006), Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (15th ruler, 2006–2020), Nawaf Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (16th ruler, 2020–2023), and Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (current 17th ruler, 2023–present).
data.description: The Al-Jaber branch of Kuwait's ruling Al-Sabah family, originating from the Bani Utbah confederation and settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s. This branch has provided multiple Emirs of Kuwait since the 20th century.
data.originStory: According to oral tradition, the Al Sabah family were expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to their predatory habits of preying on caravans in Basra and trading ships in the Shatt al-Arab. Another oral tradition states they fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south, then to Zubara on Qatar's west coast, before finally settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s where they found water.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Jaber branch continues as the ruling family of Kuwait, with Sheikh Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah serving as the current 17th ruler since 2023. They control the key government ministries including defense, interior, and foreign affairs.

data.history: The Al Refai family name derives from the Rifaʿi Sufi order (Rifaʿiyya) founded by Iraqi saint Ahmad al-Rifaʿi (c. 1119-1183) in the marshlands between Wasit and Basra. The order spread from Iraq to Syria, Egypt, and the broader Levant by the 12th-13th centuries, with the surname al-Rifaʿi denoting both affiliation to the Sufi order and claimed descent from the saint. Many contemporary bearers claim Husaynid Sayyid lineage through Ahmad al-Rifaʿi, though modern genealogical scholarship urges caution regarding uninterrupted descent claims over several centuries. The family name is widely attested across the Arab East (Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, Palestine, Egypt) and in broader diasporas, paralleling the order's historical spread. In Egypt, the monumental Al-Rifa'i Mosque in Cairo (1869-1912) memorializes this heritage and served as a royal mausoleum. Notable modern figures include political leaders like Jordanian Prime Ministers Samir al-Rifai, Zaid al-Rifai, and Samir Zaid al-Rifai, Syrian Grand Mufti Osama al-Rifai, Lebanese Prime Minister Nureddine El-Rifai, and Kuwaiti mountain climber Zed Al Refai who became the first Arab to summit Mount Everest.
data.description: The Al Refai family is a widely dispersed Arab lineage historically connected to the Rifaʿi Sufi order founded by Ahmad al-Rifaʿi in 12th-century Iraq, with many branches claiming Husaynid Sayyid descent and prominent representatives across politics, religion, and other fields in multiple Arab countries.
data.originStory: According to Sufi biographical traditions, the family name originates from Ahmad al-Rifaʿi (1119-1183), who lived and taught around Umm Ubayda near Wasit in Iraq and founded the Rifaʿi Sufi order. Standard accounts describe him as a Husayni Sayyid through Imam Musa al-Kazim, though modern historians treat such genealogical chains with methodological caution.
data.modernStatus: The Al Refai family has produced numerous prominent political leaders, particularly in Jordan where multiple family members have served as Prime Ministers, as well as religious leaders, businesspeople, athletes, and cultural figures across the Arab world. The family maintains organized associations in some regions, such as the Al Rifai Family Association in Lebanon's Baalbek-Hermel region.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Rifaʿi is a nisba derived from the eponymous saint Ahmad al-Rifaʿi and his Sufi order, the Rifaʿiyya. In Egypt and the Levant, the surname denotes both affiliation to the Sufi order and claimed descent from the saint.

data.history: The Al Shahwani are a Baloch tribe, with the name derived from the Arabic term shahwānī (شهواني), meaning 'luxurious', 'voluptuous', 'sensual', 'full' or 'loaded'. The tribe has established a significant presence in Qatar, with documented involvement in government administration and military leadership. Salem Abdullah Al-Shahwani participated in Qatar's first municipal elections in 1999 for constituency 27 in Al Thakhira, receiving 19.3% of votes as runner-up candidate. The family has maintained prominent positions in Qatar's modern governmental structure, with Major General Hazza bin Khalil al-Shahwani serving as Commander of the Qatar Amiri Guard. In the 2012 Villaggio Mall fire incident, Mansour Nasir Fazza al-Shahwani, a Ministry of Business and Trade employee who issued permits to the Gympanzee nursery, was convicted of forgery and sentenced to five years in prison, later reduced to a one-year suspended sentence.

data.history: The Al Wardi family has deep historical roots tracing back to medieval Arab scholarship and geography. The most documented early members include the 14th-century Syrian scholars Ibn al-Wardi (grandfather, 1291-1349) and his grandson Ibn al-Wardi (died c. 1446), both prominent historians and geographers from Maarat al-Numan, Syria. The elder Ibn al-Wardi was a historian and poet who died in Aleppo during the Black Death plague in 1349, while his grandson was a geographer who authored the influential work 'Kharīdat al-ʿAjā'ib wa farīdat al-gha'rāib' (The Pearl of Wonders) around 1419. According to the historical sources, their family descended from Caliph Abu Bakr, establishing their noble Arab lineage. In modern times, the Al Wardi name gained prominence through the Iraqi sociologist Ali Hussain Muhsin Al-Wardi (1913-1995), born in Kadhimiya, Baghdad, who became one of Iraq's most influential social historians and intellectuals. The contemporary period has also seen notable figures like the Persian-born Saudi musician Alaa Wardi (born 1987 in Riyadh to Iranian parents), demonstrating the diverse geographic and ethnic branches that carry the Al Wardi surname today.
data.description: Al Wardi is a historically significant Arab family name with documented scholarly lineage dating to 14th-century Syria, later spreading across the Middle East with notable branches in Iraq and modern Gulf states.
data.originStory: The Al Wardi family traces its origins to medieval Syria, specifically Maarat al-Numan, where the prominent 14th-century scholars Ibn al-Wardi (grandfather and grandson) established the family's reputation for learning and scholarship. Historical sources indicate their lineage descended from Caliph Abu Bakr, giving them noble Arab ancestry.
data.modernStatus: The Al Wardi name appears across multiple countries and professions, with branches including Iraqi academic families, Iranian-origin Gulf residents, and various professional and cultural figures throughout the Middle East.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'al-Wardi' appears to derive from the Arabic root related to 'ward' (وردي), though the specific etymological origin is not detailed in the available sources.

data.history: The Al Amoodi family traces its origins to the 13th century Sufi saint Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi, a Hadhrami Mashaikh family from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen. Sa'eed ibn Isa was known as 'Amoududdin' (pillar of the religion) due to his prolonged standing in prayer. The family descends from Abu Bakr As-Siddiq, the first Muslim Caliph, through his eldest son Abdurrahman in the ninth generation. The Al-Amoudi family established themselves as one of the most important Mashaikh families in Tarim, Yemen, becoming successful traders throughout the Middle East. In modern times, family members have migrated to various Gulf countries, with notable figures emerging in Saudi Arabia and the UAE. The family has produced prominent journalists, scholars, government officials, and professional athletes, maintaining their scholarly and commercial traditions across generations.
data.description: The Al Amoodi (Al-Amoudi) family is a Hadhrami Mashaikh family descending from 13th century Sufi saint Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi, with origins tracing back to Abu Bakr As-Siddiq. data.history: The Al Shammakh family traces its origins to the ancient Arabian poet al-Shammākh ibn Ḍirār, who was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic poet from the tribe of Banū Tha'laba. Al-Shammākh lived during the transition period from the Jahiliyyah (pre-Islamic era) to early Islam and was considered among the third tier of pre-Islamic poets according to Ibn Sallām al-Jumaḥī. The poet was known for his literary works and engaged in notable poetic exchanges with other renowned poets of his time, including al-Nābigha al-Ja'dī and Jabal ibn Jawwal. His poetry was studied and critiqued by later Islamic scholars, particularly the famous 9th-century grammarian Tha'lab (Abū al-'Abbās Ahmad ibn Yahyā), who analyzed al-Shammākh's verses in scholarly gatherings attended by other prominent philologists of Baghdad. The family name appears to derive from this ancestral poet, suggesting a lineage that has maintained its connection to this literary heritage through the centuries. While the exact migration patterns of the family from ancient Arabian territories to modern locations are not documented in the available sources, the preservation of the name indicates a continued identification with this poetic and tribal ancestry.
data.description: The Al Shammakh family traces its lineage to the ancient Arabian poet al-Shammākh ibn Ḍirār of the Banū Tha'laba tribe, a prominent literary figure from the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. The family maintains connection to this ancestral poetic heritage.
data.originStory: The family originates from the lineage of al-Shammākh ibn Ḍirār, a renowned poet from the Banū Tha'laba tribe who lived during the transition from pre-Islamic times to early Islam and was recognized as one of the notable poets of his era.

In more recent times, the family name is associated with Syrian architect Marwa Al-Sabouni, born in 1981 in Homs, who has gained international recognition for her architectural theory and writing about the relationship between urban design and social conflict. The contemporary branch represented by Marwa Al-Sabouni has remained in Syria through the civil war, with her work focusing on how architecture can contribute to peace and social cohesion.

The family name also appears in historical records of Oman, specifically in the Bawarh village of Al Suwaiq wilayat in Al Batinah North Governorate, where Al Hindasi is mentioned among the tribes residing alongside Al Barhi, Al Balushi, Al Qarini, Al Maqbali, Al Daba'oni, and Al 'Araymi tribes. Additionally, the Al Hindasi name appears in contemporary military service, with a soldier named Al Hindasi being documented as serving with the United Arab Emirates Armed Forces during the Yemen War in 2018, where he was involved in combat operations alongside fellow Emirati soldiers Ali Al-mismari and Bilal Al Saadi.

data.history: The Al Kitbi family descends from the Bani Qitab tribe (Arabic: بني كتب), an influential Arab tribe of the UAE and Oman. The tribe consisted of both settled southern and nomadic northern sections, with the northern branch primarily settling in the inland towns of Dhaid and Al Falayah. At the turn of the 19th century, the tribe comprised approximately 2,100 nomadic Bedouin and 2,700 settled people, with their territory stretching from south of the Buraimi oasis to the eastern foothills of the Hajar Mountains, the Jiri plain north of Sharjah, and the fertile areas around Dhaid. The southern Bani Qitab, consisting of some 500 households, settled around the village of Aflaj Bani Qitab in the Dhahirah area, which later became part of Oman. The family has produced numerous notable figures including Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi, third wife of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, Dr. Ebtesam Al-Ketbi, a prominent political scientist and think tank leader, and Colonel Sultan Mohammed Ali al-Kitbi, who was killed in Yemen in 2015.

data.originStory: The family originates from the Bani Qitab tribe, which was historically divided into nomadic northern and settled southern sections, with territories spanning from the Buraimi oasis to the Hajar Mountains and including the fertile areas around Sharjah's inland towns.

data.history: The Al Khudhair family's documented history centers primarily around Adel Khudhair (born 1954 in Basra), an Iraqi footballer and coach who became one of the most notable figures bearing this surname. Born in Basra, Adel Khudhair played as a defender and midfielder, representing Iraq in international competitions including the 1980 Moscow Olympics and the 1979 Arabian Gulf Cup where he scored two goals. His career was significantly impacted by political persecution when the Iraqi Ba'athist government removed him from the national team in 1982 after discovering his brother Shaker belonged to the Dawa party, leading to his brother's assassination along with his nephew Mahmoud, and Khudhair's subsequent house arrest. During the 1991 uprising in Basra, Khudhair participated in military operations and was seriously injured, leading to his treatment in Kuwait and Iran before joining the Iraqi opposition through the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan until the 2003 invasion. After 2003, he established a successful coaching career in Bahrain, training clubs including Al-Malkiya, Al-Tadhamun, Al-Etifaq, and East Riffa Club, and later returned to Iraq to coach Al-Hawija SC, leading them to promotion to the Iraqi First Division League in 2022.

The name appears to be associated with Iraqi football and coaching circles, particularly in Basra and later Bahrain.

data.modernStatus: The family is represented in professional football coaching, particularly in the Gulf region. Adel Khudhair has established a reputation as a professional coach in Bahrain since 2003, working with multiple clubs and achieving notable success including team promotions.

data.history: The Al Khadher family is a prominent Kuwaiti military family, with Lieutenant General Mohammed Khaled Al-Khadher serving as the former Chief of General Staff of Kuwait Armed Forces. Born on October 4, 1955, in Alshamiyah, Mohammed Khaled lived his childhood in Jiblah and Al-Murqab before his family moved to Alfaiha in 1960, where he continues to reside. The family has a strong military tradition, with Mohammed Khaled's three younger brothers following his footsteps into military service. The family also produced notable figures in the arts, including the famous melodist Rashed Khaled Al-Khadher, who died in January 1998. Mohammed Khaled's military career began in 1977 when he was commissioned from Kuwait's military academy as part of the 8th intake, progressing through various command positions to become Chief of General Staff, demonstrating the family's sustained prominence in Kuwait's military establishment.

data.modernStatus: The family maintains high status in Kuwait's military establishment, with Mohammed Khaled Al-Khadher having served as Chief of General Staff and receiving multiple extensions due to his distinguished service. data.history: The Dulaim (also rendered as Dulaimi, Al Duliam or Dulaym) is an Arab royal tribe with over three million members, with history dating back to pre-Islamic times. According to German orientalist Max von Oppenheim and Iraqi historian Mohammed bin Hamad Al-Bassam Al-Tamimi (1831), the Dulaim tribe originally divided into four groups: Al-Bu Ridaini, Al-Bu Fahd, Al-Bu Alwan, and Al-Mahamda. The tribe is considered the largest Sunni Arab tribe in Iraq, living on the Euphrates from below Al Hillah and southern Baghdad to Fallujah, Ramadi, al-Qaim, Samarra and Mosul. Originally Bedouins living between Ramadi and Al-Qa'im, at the end of the fifteenth century the clans began migration eastward until they arrived at Fallujah and south of Baghdad. The Principality of the Dulaim tribe (Amirate Al-Dulaim) was almost a state of self-rule that flourished in the eighteenth century, controlling areas and subjecting other tribes in the region. During World War I, Sheikh Ali Sulaiman made submission to the British in September 1917, though many clans continued assisting the Ottomans until British occupation. The tribe played significant roles in founding modern Iraq, contributing to stability during the monarchy period and forming 20-30% of the Iraqi Army during the Saddam era.
data.description: The Dulaim is the largest Sunni Arab tribe in Iraq with over three million members, historically based along the Euphrates River from Baghdad to the Syrian border. The tribe has branched into more than 1,000 clans and maintains significant political and social influence in western Iraq, particularly in Al Anbar Governorate.
data.originStory: Some scholars believe the Dulaims were part of the first Arab Conquests in the 7th century. The tribe's documented history shows they were originally Bedouins living between Ramadi and Al-Qa'im before migrating eastward in the late 15th century.
data.modernStatus: The Dulaim tribe forms the majority population in Al Anbar Governorate and has significant representation in Iraqi politics. Current notable political figures include Sheikh Ali Hatim al-Suleiman Al-Assafi Al-Dulaimi as prince of the tribe, and various sheikhs leading the major clans. The tribe has been involved in post-2003 Iraqi politics through various parties and coalitions.

data.history: The Al Mirdha family appears to have historical connections to the Indian subcontinent, as evidenced by the mention of 'Mirdha Ikram' in the courtyard of Nizamuddin Auliya's dargah in Delhi, where 14th-century historian Ziauddin Barani's gravestone is located. The Mirdha are documented as a Jat clan primarily found in the Marwar region of Rajasthan, India, with their most prominent political dynasty originating from Kuchera village in Nagaur district. The family gained political prominence through leaders like Baldev Ram Mirdha (1889-1953), known as 'Kisan Kesari' (Lion of the Farmers) for his leadership in farmers' movements, and his son Ram Niwas Mirdha (1924-2010), who served as the longest-serving Speaker of Rajasthan Legislative Assembly and held multiple Union ministerial positions. The surname 'Mirdha' originated as a title for managing the postal system in Marwar, known as the 'Mirdha Dak,' during the pre-independence era. The family has maintained significant influence in Rajasthani politics across multiple generations, with members holding positions in state and national government.

data.originStory: The Mirdha family originated from Kuchera village in Nagaur district, Rajasthan. The surname derives from a title awarded for managing the postal system in Marwar, called the 'Mirdha Dak,' during the pre-independence period.
data.modernStatus: The Mirdha family remains politically influential in Rajasthan, with members having served in various high-ranking positions including state legislature, Union ministries, and cultural institutions.

data.history: The Abdulhadi family name appears across multiple contexts in Gulf region documentation, though no unified family genealogy is established in the research. The most prominent contemporary figure is Abdulhadi Mana Al-Hajri, a Qatari businessman born in December 1979, who is the son of Mana bin Abdul Hadi Al Hajri, Qatar's former ambassador to Jordan (2003-2011). Al-Hajri became internationally notable through major property acquisitions, including the £700 million purchase of London's Ritz Hotel in March 2020, making it the world's most expensive hotel sale. His sister Al-Anoud bint Mana Al Hajri married Qatar's Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani as his second wife, establishing significant political connections. In Bahrain, the name appears in activism contexts with Abdulhadi al-Khawaja, a prominent human rights activist and co-founder of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights, who was sentenced to life imprisonment in 2011 during the Bahraini uprising. The research also documents Palestinian techno DJ Samaʼ Abdulhadi, born in Jordan in 1990 to a family deported from Palestine, indicating the name's presence across different Arab communities and contexts.

data.history:
The Taher family name appears in multiple distinct contexts across different regions and time periods. The earliest documented use comes from the Persian Abū Ṭāher family of Kāšān, who were prominent potters active between 1205 and 1333 CE. This family produced four generations of master craftsmen whose signatures appear on surviving works, particularly tiled miḥrābs of mosques. The family began with Bū Ṭāher Ḥosayn in the late 12th century and continued through Moḥammad ibn Abī Ṭāher (1205-1215), who collaborated on major shrine projects at Qom and Mašhad, followed by his descendants ʿAlī, Yūsof, and Abu'l-Qāsem, the latter being both a craftsman and historian under the Mongol Il-khan Olǰāytū.

In the modern Gulf context, the name appears again with Dr. Abdulhadi H. Taher, a prominent Saudi Arabian figure who served as former governor of the Saudi Petroleum and Mineral organization and board member of Aramco. The Taher family became major shareholders in Gulf Oil International before the Hinduja Group acquired a large share from them. Additionally, Naser Taher, born in Jordan in 1952, represents another branch, founding MultiBank Group in Dubai in 2005 after working with major global financial institutions and serving in various advisory roles to Chinese financial authorities.

The research also references a Yaseinn Taher from Buffalo, New York, who was part of the Lackawanna Six case in 2002. This individual, born to a family described as not devoutly Muslim but observant of certain traditions, became involved in a controversial legal case related to attending training camps in Afghanistan before 9/11. His story illustrates the complex identity negotiations faced by Muslim-Americans in the early 2000s, as he struggled between his Muslim faith and American identity while raising questions about religious obligations versus national loyalty.
data.description: The Taher name appears across multiple unrelated family lines, from medieval Persian craftsmen in Kashan to modern Gulf business leaders and American Muslims, with no confirmed genealogical connections between the various branches.
data.modernStatus: The Gulf branch includes major stakeholders in international oil and financial services, with the Saudi Taher family having significant holdings in Gulf Oil International before selling to the Hinduja Group, and Naser Taher operating MultiBank Group from Dubai

data.history: The research provided focuses on Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum of the Al Maktoum family, not the Al Mohd family. The Al Maktoum family is Dubai's ruling family and descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, with Mohammed serving as the tribal leader of Al-Falasi. Sheikh Mohammed was born on 15 July 1949 as the third of four sons of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, ruler of Dubai. His mother was Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan, daughter of former ruler of Abu Dhabi Sheikh Hamdan bin Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. The Al Maktoum family has ruled Dubai continuously, with Mohammed becoming ruler in 2006 following his brother Maktoum's death. Under Mohammed's leadership, Dubai transformed into a global city through major development projects and government-owned enterprises. However, the research contains no information about a family called 'Al Mohd' - this appears to be a different family entirely from the Al Maktoum lineage documented in the research.
data.description: No information available about the Al Mohd family in the provided research, which focuses exclusively on the Al Maktoum family of Dubai.

data.history: The Al Hawaaj family is identified in Wikipedia as a branch of the Banu Khuza'ah tribe (Āl Ḥawāj/al-Ḥawāj), specifically located in the Al-Aḥsā Province of Saudi Arabia. The Banu Khuza'ah are an ancient Azdite, Qahtanite tribe that historically ruled Mecca as custodians of the Kaaba for approximately 500 years before the time of Prophet Muhammad. The tribe's origins are disputed among genealogists, with some tracing them to Qahtanite descent through Amr ibn Luhay from the Azd tribe of Yemen, while others claim Adnanite descent through Muḍar. The Banu Khuza'ah played significant roles in early Islamic history, including forming alliances with Prophet Muhammad and participating in the conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. Members of the broader Khuza'ah confederation established the Kingdom of Khaza'il in Iraq from 1534-1921, ruling the Middle and Lower Euphrates region with significant autonomy from Ottoman rule. Today, Al Hawaaj descendants of this ancient tribe continue to reside in Al-Aḥsā Province, maintaining their connection to one of Arabia's most historically significant tribal lineages. The family name appears in a modern context through Ibrahim bin Hassan Al Hawaj, who was appointed as Bahrain's Minister of Works in June 2022.
data.description: Al Hawaaj is a family branch of the ancient Banu Khuza'ah tribe, historically significant as former custodians of Mecca, currently residing in Saudi Arabia's Al-Aḥsā Province. A notable modern member serves as Bahrain's Minister of Works.
data.originStory: The Al Hawaaj trace their lineage to the Banu Khuza'ah, one of the main ancestral tribes of Arabia. According to traditional accounts, their ancestor Amr ibn Luhay migrated from Yemen northward around the 5th century CE, settling around Mecca where they became custodians of the Kaaba for 500 years before Islam.
data.modernStatus: Members of the Al Hawaaj family continue to reside in Al-Aḥsā Province, Saudi Arabia. Ibrahim bin Hassan Al Hawaj serves as Bahrain's Minister of Works, appointed in June 2022, overseeing major infrastructure projects including the North Manama Causeway Phase 2.
data.nameEtymology: The name Khuza'ah, from which Al Hawaaj derives, comes from the Arabic phrase 'khaza'a 'an aṣḥābihi' meaning 'he has lagged behind his companions,' referring to how they stayed behind when other tribes migrated from Marib after the dam breach.

data.history: The Al-Houthi family is an aristocratic Yemeni tribe who trace their origins to the Banu Hashim, descending directly from Hasan ibn Ali. The family being part of the Hashemite Sadah social class of North Yemen bears a common ancestor to other Zaydi Hashemite families in the region, all being descended from Yahya ibn Husayn al-Rassi al-Hasani who arrived in Sa'ada in 894 AH. He was a follower of Zaydi Shi'ism and gave rise to the Hadawi school of thought within it. The clan is large in number and is mainly based in the Marran Mountains of Sa'ada Province in North-Western Yemen, where they traditionally presided as Qadis in the Sa'ada region until the 1962 civil war in Yemen which resulted in the overthrow of the Mutawakkilite Kingdom. After the end of Zaydi rule from the 1960s onwards, Zaydis faced discrimination and Sunnification policies from consequent Sunni dominated governments. The Al-Houthi family has produced numerous Ulama of the Zaydi Shia community and became politically active in the late 20th century, with members founding the Hizb al-Haqq party. Following political marginalization and increasing tensions with Salafi groups, the family established the Houthi movement in the 2000s, which led to armed conflict and eventually control of significant parts of Yemen including the capital Sanaa in 2014.
data.description: The Al-Houthi family is an aristocratic Yemeni Hashemite tribe based in the Marran Mountains of Sa'ada Province, tracing their lineage to Hasan ibn Ali. They have led the Houthi movement since the early 2000s and currently control much of northern Yemen including the capital Sanaa.
data.originStory: The Al-Houthi family traces their origins to the Banu Hashim, descending directly from Hasan ibn Ali. They are part of the Hashemite Sadah social class of North Yemen, descended from Yahya ibn Husayn al-Rassi al-Hasani who arrived in Sa'ada in 894 AH as a follower of Zaydi Shi'ism.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Houthi family leads the Houthi movement (Ansar Allah) which currently controls the capital Sanaa and all of former North Yemen except for eastern Marib Governorate. The movement is led by Abdul-Malik al-Houthi and is engaged in ongoing civil war against the internationally recognized government.

data.history: The Al Ahsai family traces its origins to the prominent Abi Jumhur clan, a branch of the Banu Shayban tribe in Al-Ahsa. According to scholar Jawad al-Ramadan, the Abi Jumhur are considered ancestors of the Al-Aithan family. The family produced notable Islamic scholars during the 15th century, particularly Mohammed bin Ali bin Ibrahim Al-Shaybani Al-Bakri Al-Ahsa'i, known as Ibn Abi Jumhur al-Ahsa'i (1435-1505). His father Zain al-Din Ali and grandfather Husam al-Din Ibrahim were both respected Shi'ite scholars who belonged to this prominent family in Al-Ahsa. The family was established in the village of Taymiyyah in what is now Al-Ahsa Governorate during the reign of the first Jabrid Emir. Ibn Abi Jumhur became an influential Shia Muslim scholar who adhered to the Ja'fari school of Islamic jurisprudence and gained renown throughout the Islamic world for his debates and writings on fiqh, philosophy, and hadith interpretation. A mosque in Taymiyyah is attributed to Ibn Abi Jumhur's time and includes an engraving in the mihrab dated to 1407. In 2013, the Ibn Abi Jumhur Heritage Society was founded in Qom as a non-profit dedicated to historic preservation, demonstrating the continued recognition of the family's scholarly legacy.
data.description: Al Ahsai is a scholarly family from Al-Ahsa descended from the Abi Jumhur clan of the Banu Shayban tribe, known for producing prominent Islamic scholars including the influential 15th-century jurist Ibn Abi Jumhur al-Ahsa'i.
data.originStory: The Al Ahsai family descended from the Abi Jumhur clan, which was a branch in Al-Ahsa of the Banu Shayban tribe, itself a scion of the Banu Bakr. The family established itself in the village of Taymiyyah in Al-Ahsa Governorate during the Jabrid period.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains its scholarly legacy through the Ibn Abi Jumhur Heritage Society founded in Qom in 2013, dedicated to historic preservation of their ancestral contributions to Islamic scholarship.
data.nameEtymology: Al Ahsai refers to their origin from Al-Ahsa region in Eastern Arabia

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is limited verified information about the Al Amry family. The most concrete reference appears in relation to El Amry Farouk (1970–2024), described as an Egyptian businessman and politician. Additionally, there is a reference to an El-Amry Minaret in Esna, Egypt, built in 1081 by Sa'd al-Dawla al-Qawwasi as part of a historic mosque established between 474 and 476 hijri (1081 to 1084 AD) by Badr El Deen Gamaly during the Fatimid Era. The minaret remains the only surviving part of the original mosque, which was demolished and rebuilt in modern style in the 1960s. There is also mention of Mohamed Saeed Al-Amry, a 25-year-old Saudi national who was captured in Madinah in 2005 as part of counter-terrorism operations. However, the research does not provide sufficient information to establish a comprehensive family history, origins, or connections between these individuals bearing the Al Amry name.

data.history: The Al Badwawi family is documented primarily through the prominent human rights activist Khalfan al-Badwawi, born in Muscat, Oman. Khalfan's father previously worked in Kuwait, where he was exposed to pan-Arab political movements before returning to Oman, suggesting the family had some mobility within the Gulf region. The family appears to be middle-class, with Khalfan pursuing education and professional careers in nursing and healthcare before becoming involved in political activism during the 2011-2012 Omani pro-democracy movement. The family name also appears in Emirati contexts, with Ali Hamad Madhad Saif Al-Badwawi serving as a FIFA referee from the UAE, though any connection between these individuals is not established in the available research. The research does not provide information about the family's tribal origins, historical migration patterns, or broader genealogical connections within the GCC region.
data.description: A family name found in Oman and UAE contexts, documented through individuals in activism and sports officiating, with limited historical information available about their origins or tribal affiliations.
